D10 Imaging L5 Evaluate Reference

Skin-Tone Line on the Vectorscope

human skin of every ethnicity clusters along a single line in I-Q space at ~123°; the vectorscope's I axis is built around this empirical fact

The NTSC I/Q encoding (1953) placed the orange-cyan axis (I) along the dominant skin-tone direction because broadcast bandwidth was scarce — and the eye is least tolerant of bad skin. Modern grading carries this forward as the “skin tone line” on a vectorscope. The line runs at ≈ 123° (from red toward yellow), and skin patches of every ethnicity cluster on it.

Cycle the skin sample. The marker stays on the line — what changes is its distance from centre (saturation) and angle from red (hue). Use the WB slider to push a global colour cast and watch the cluster rotate off the line — exactly what a vectorscope flags when skin grading drifts.
